data.title: Hyundai Motor to Introduce Ioniq, N Vision Models in Asphalt Legends Video Game via Gameloft Partnership
data.description:
Hyundai Motor partnered with French video game company Gameloft's racing game, Asphalt Legends Unite, to introduce its Ioniq 5 NEV and N Vision 74 hydrogen fuel cell-hybrid concept to digital tracks.

The initiative, launching with Update 43 on April 2, is part of Hyundai's strategy to engage Gen Z and Alpha gamers through content marketing, the automaker said Wednesday.

Shares of Hyundai Motor fell more than 1% in recent trade on Thursday.

data.title: Keppel Divests Entire Stake in Keppel Philippines
data.description:
Keppel divested its entire 29.52% direct stake in the outstanding capital stock of Keppel Philippines Holdings to Kepwealth for 462.9 million Philippines pesos, according to a Wednesday filing with the Singapore Exchange.

The company does not expect the divestment to materially impact its earnings for the current financial year.

data.title: AI Platform Magai Expands Team, Reaches Key Milestone Amid Growing Demand
data.description:
Magai is a US-based platform led by entrepreneur Dustin Stout, seeking to democratize access to artificial intelligence and transform how people interact with technology

Bakersfield, California--(Newsfile Corp. - April 2, 2025) - Magai, the US-based AI platform built to simplify access to artificial intelligence, has grown its team and crossed a key revenue milestone following a wave of adoption among creative professionals and consultants.

AI Platform Magai Marks New Milestones Following Team Expansion, Vows to Continue Efforts to Revolutionize How People Safely Access AI

To view an enhanced version of this graphic, please visit:

https://images.newsfilecorp.com/files/8814/243520_6d91ff63e4078a5e_001full.jpg

Founded by Dustin Stout, Magai was initially operated solo. The team has now expanded to six, with recent hires in customer support, development, and content operations. Stout says the new members have helped accelerate both product growth and user support as the platform scales.

"Each addition brought unique strengths," said Stout. "Their contributions helped us move faster and serve users better-especially as Magai gained traction with agencies and consultants who needed an intuitive, reliable AI solution."

Shortly after the expansion, Magai reached one of its highest-performing periods, generating over $114,000 in just two weeks. The platform attributes this growth to refinements in user experience and a broader toolset that combines AI chat, image generation, and video creation into one interface.

Magai's growth comes as more professionals turn to unified platforms that streamline AI usage without requiring technical expertise. The team says further updates are on the horizon, with a continued focus on usability and accessibility.

More information about Magai and the services it covers is available on the official website.

About Magai

Magai is an all-in-one AI platform that brings together powerful AI tools for chat, image generation, and video creation in a single, intuitive interface. Designed to make artificial intelligence accessible and safe for everyday users, Magai serves content creators, agencies, and consultants who want efficient, user-friendly solutions without the technical complexity.

Contact Info:

Name: Dustin W. Stout

Email: hello@magai.co

Organization: Magai

Website: https://magai.co

To view the source version of this press release, please visit https://www.newsfilecorp.com/release/243520

data.title: Market Chatter: Offers for Stake in Eni's Plenitude Values Renewables Business Up to EUR13 Billion
data.description:
Eni's (ENI.MI) sale of a 15% stake in Plenitude attracted offers valuing the renewables business between 12 billion euros and 13 billion euros, Bloomberg News reported Wednesday, citing people familiar with the matter.

The unnamed sources said the business has piqued the interest of potential buyers, including Apollo Global Management, Ares Management, and Stonepeak. They added that final bids are expected in the coming weeks.

The Italian energy company's ownership of Plenitude decreased to 90% after selling a 10% stake earlier in the week to Energy Infrastructure Partners at a valuation of 10 billion euros, including debt. According to people, deliberations on the final valuation for the next sale continue,, and the figure could still change.

Eni did not immediately respond to a request for comment from MT Newswires.

data.title: King Wan Secures Contracts Worth SG$40.4 Million; Shares Up 3%
data.description:
King Wan secured mechanical and electrical contracts worth around SG$40.4 million between January and March, according to a filing with the Singapore Exchange on Wednesday.

The projects are slated for completion in 2028.

Shares of the company were up nearly 3% in recent trading.

data.title: Leader Environmental Gets Singapore Bourse Nod For AIWater Disposal
data.description:
Leader Environmental Technologies has secured approval from the Singapore Exchange regarding its proposed disposal of the AIWater Group and the associated conditional distribution to shareholders, according to a bourse filing on Wednesday.

The proposed transaction involves the disposal of AIWater and the distribution of up to 238.7 million yuan to its shareholders.

Additionally, up to 34.7 million yuan will be paid to InnoVision, holder of redeemable zero-coupon convertible bonds.

data.title: IMF sees Japan sustainably hitting central bank's inflation target
data.description:
Key points:
  • Risks to Japan's growth tilted to downside, IMF says
  • Risks to inflation broadly balanced, food inflation to moderate
  • IMF urges BOJ to keep phasing out monetary support
  • BOJ must raise rates in 'data-dependent, flexible' manner

Japan is showing signs of sustainably achieving the central bank's 2% inflation target with robust consumption and capital expenditure likely to underpin the economy, the International Monetary Fund said on Wednesday.

"After three decades of near-zero inflation, signs are growing that Japan's economy can reach a new equilibrium with inflation sustained at the Bank of Japan's 2% headline inflation target and growth at the 0.5% potential," the IMF said in a statement released after concluding its annual consultations with Japan.

"Risks to growth are tilted to the downside, while risks to inflation are broadly balanced," the statement said, adding the chance of a global economic slowdown and weakening of domestic consumption were among downside risks to economic growth.

The IMF's statement, released in Washington before U.S. President Donald Trump's announcement of global reciprocal tariffs on Wednesday, did not mention the potential impact of the 24% levy the U.S. will place on imports from Japan.

Japan's headline inflation stood at 3.7% in February, staying above the BOJ's target for nearly three years, as stubbornly high food costs hurt households.

While rising food and energy costs posed upside risks to inflation, they were likely to moderate ahead and help Japan's inflation converge to the BOJ's target, the IMF said.

In concluding the consultations with Japan, the IMF's executive directors urged the BOJ to gradually withdraw monetary support if Japan's economy moves in line with the group's staff forecasts, according to the statement.

They "urged the BOJ to remain data‑dependent and flexible, and to continue its clear communication" in raising interest rates given uncertainty over the level of Japan's "neutral" rate that neither cools nor overheats growth, it said.

The IMF expects Japan's economy to expand 1.2% in 2025, after a 0.1% increase in 2024, as robust corporate profits and prospects of higher wages underpin private spending. It projects headline inflation to slow to 2.4% in 2025 from 2.7% last year.

The BOJ exited a decade-long stimulus programme last year and raised interest rates to 0.5% in January on the view Japan was on the cusp of sustainably hitting its inflation target.

Governor Kazuo Ueda has said the BOJ will keep pushing up borrowing costs if continued wage gains underpin consumption and allow firms to raise prices.

data.title: Markets Expected U.S. Trade Demands to Soften. There's No Sign of That YetMarket Talk
data.description: Earlier this year, many investors were expecting President Trump's tough trade demands would soften over time, says Damien Hennessy, Zenith Investment Partners head of asset allocation. "However, this somewhat comfortable working assumption has been challenged by the escalation in announced tariffs," he says. The latest tariff plan, "together with the collateral damage we've already seen on consumer and business confidence, and the disappearance of the so-called 'Trump put', has sent markets into a tailspin." Melbourne-based Hennessy says Zenith has raised its estimated probability of U.S. recession to 35%, from 20%, "a level that cannot be ignored." (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

data.title: Zhongjing Petrochemical recommences production at No.3 PP unit
data.description: Zhongjing Petrochemical has restarted its No.3 Polypropylene (PP) unit in Fuqing.A Polymerupdate source in China informed a team member,

data.title: Siemens to Buy Scientific Software Company Dotmatics for $5 Billion
data.description:
Siemens signed a deal to purchase US-based scientific software company Dotmatics for $5.1 billion from Insight Partners, according to a Wednesday release.

The deal, which will expand the German technology group's artificial intelligence-powered product lifecycle management software portfolio into life sciences, is anticipated to be closed in the first half of fiscal 2026, pending regulatory approvals and customary closing conditions.

data.title: Suzuki Motor - All Japan Assembly Plants To Continue Normal Operations Beyond Thursday After Partial Suspension Due To Parts Supply Disruption
data.description:
Suzuki Motor Corp :

  • ALL JAPAN ASSEMBLY PLANTS TO CONTINUE NORMAL OPERATIONS BEYOND THURSDAY AFTER PARTIAL SUSPENSION DUE TO PARTS SUPPLY DISRUPTION

data.title: Japan Keeps All Options Open in Response to Trump’s Tariffs
data.description:
Japan’s Trade Minister Yoji Muto said on Thursday that all options remain open in response to President Trump’s newly announced tariffs.

He called the decision “extremely regrettable” and reiterated Japan’s request for an exemption during a meeting with U.S. officials.

Muto emphasized the need for a careful yet decisive response and announced a task force to assess the impact of the 24% tariff on Japanese goods.

A previously planned 25% tariff on car imports will take effect as scheduled, dealing a significant blow to Japan’s auto industry.

Tokyo also expressed “serious concerns” about the tariffs’ compliance with World Trade Organization rules.

data.title: ICYMI - China will raise the retail prices of gasoline and diesel from today
data.description:
China will increase retail prices of gasoline and diesel starting Thursday, following recent rises in international crude oil prices, according to the National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC).

The price of gasoline will rise by 230 yuan (approximately USD 32) per tonne, while diesel will increase by 220 yuan per tonne.

The NDRC has called on major state-owned oil companies — including CNPC, Sinopec, and CNOOC — along with other refineries, to maintain steady production and ensure smooth distribution in order to support stable fuel supply.

Under China’s pricing system, domestic fuel prices are adjusted in line with global oil price movements.

data.title: Trump Tariffs Set to Hit Asian Exports HardMarket Talk
data.description: Trump's tariffs are arguably much higher than anticipated by the market and will affect Asian exports, says Chris Kushlis, chief emerging markets macro strategist at T. Rowe Price, in an email. Tariff increases in the range of 20%-35% will pose a meaningful headwind to growth this year, especially for more open, trade-oriented economies, he says. A relatively high proportion of many Asian economies' export value ends up in the U.S., so the broad application of tariffs globally will hinder effects to redirect trade, he adds. Risk assets will likely remain under pressure and volatile as companies assess the impact of tariffs on their business plans and the potential time frame for negotiations to cut tariffs, he says. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0312 GMT - Trump's tariffs are arguably much higher than anticipated by the market and will affect Asian exports, says Chris Kushlis, chief emerging markets macro strategist at T. Rowe Price, in an email. Tariff increases in the range of 20%-35% will pose a meaningful headwind to growth this year, especially for more open, trade-oriented economies, he says. A relatively high proportion of many Asian economies' export value ends up in the U.S., so the broad application of tariffs globally will hinder effects to redirect trade, he adds. Risk assets will likely remain under pressure and volatile as companies assess the impact of tariffs on their business plans and the potential time frame for negotiations to cut tariffs, he says. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0311 GMT - Trump's higher-than-expected tariffs on Chinese products may prompt more forceful retaliation from Beijing and drag on China's growth and inflation this year, says Lynn Song, economist at ING. While Song maintains his forecast that U.S. tariffs likely pose a drag between 0.4-0.8 ppt on China's growth in 2025, and says in a note that this week's developments likely means the impact will be toward the higher end of that band. The potential hit from tariffs will likely prompt more monetary easing from Beijing, says Song, who says rate and RRR cuts could be delivered together for better effect and the PBOC is unlikely to intentionally devalue the yuan to offset tariffs. (singaporeeditors@dowjones.com)

0245 GMT - Australian consumers might benefit from President Trump's latest tariffs, should reduced-price surplus European and Asian goods be redirected away from the U.S. and into Australia, says Whitefield managing director Angus Gluskie. But this could be a negative for Australia's big iron ore producers, who ship vast amounts of the steel ingredient to mills in Asia, he says. Still, the Sydney-based investor reckons the greatest impact on Australia from the sweeping U.S. tariffs is likely to be from how it jolts the global economy. "Disruption and uncertainty are difficult for corporations and consumers," says Gluskie. Tariff-driven inflation in the U.S. may also drive up global interest rates, he says. "So concerns around potential global stagflation are likely to remain the larger concern for investment markets." (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0232 GMT - The sweeping Trump tariffs could pose higher-than-expected risks to China's exports, says Pinpoint Asset Management chief economist Zhiwei Zhang in a note. The high tariffs on Asean countries, such as Vietnam and Cambodia, could weigh on China's exports to these countries, he says. Supply chains in East Asia are vertically integrated as China's exports of intermediate goods are crucial for these countries' exports, he says. "Retaliation from China on the U.S. tariffs is likely coming," he says. Zhang expects Beijing to push up fiscal spending and cut interest rate and reserve requirement ratio to offset the damage to China's exports, he says. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0230 GMT - The Singapore dollar slightly strengthens against its U.S. counterpart in the morning Asian session as traders digest the U.S. tariff announcement on Wednesday. "Singapore gets the bare minimum 10%," OCBC's Global Markets Research team says in a research report. "The economies with 10% tariffs will probably be insulated compared to those with higher rates," the team says. "However, higher tariffs rates will need to be considered with the trade to the U.S., leading to asymmetric impact for economic growth and trade," the team adds. USD/SGD is 0.2% lower at 1.3433. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0210 GMT - Two-way swings in FX markets are likely, given the global response to U.S. President Trump's tariff announcement is still forming, DBS Group Research's Philip Wee says in a research report. "Some countries (are) seen retaliating and others negotiating, amid narratives looking for a deal with the U.S. and resignation to the 'new normal' on global trade," the senior FX strategist says. Also, "cautiously optimistic investor will likely point to the tariff levels as intentionally less aggressive than they could have been. Conversely, the pessimists see Trump's sweeping tariffs as confirming a hard pivot from decades of globalization towards protectionism," Wee adds. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0209 GMT - Trump's latest tariffs are set to hit Asia hard, leaving investors wondering if countries will retaliate or push for deals. China and Vietnam have seen the toughest increases and will likely find it difficult to broker a deal, Morgan Stanley economists led by Chetan Ahya say. India, Japan, South Korea and Indonesia could potentially bargain for lower tariffs, they write in a note. For Thailand, Malaysia and Taiwan, that prospect looks somewhat difficult, they add. The hit to growth will be determined by each economy's goods exports to GDP: "The more trade-oriented an economy is, the bigger the growth drag will be." (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0201 GMT - The Canadian dollar may enter another downtrend against the yen, based on the daily chart, Oanda's Kelvin Wong says in an email. The recent corrective rebound of CAD/JPY from the March 11 low may have reached a terminal point on March 26, as the currency pair's price movements shaped a bearish candlestick pattern, the senior market analyst says. Also, the daily relative strength index indicator has signaled a bearish momentum condition, which underpins the beginning of a possible downtrend, Wong says. Any break of the currency pair below support at 101.80 exposes subsequent medium-term support levels at 99.60 and 97.55, the analyst adds. CAD/JPY slips 1.1% to 103.69. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0148 GMT - Asia is generally harder hit by the U.S. tariffs announced overnight than other regions, MUFG Bank's Michael Wan says in a research report. Among the Asian markets that MUFG Bank covers, the biggest increase in reciprocal tariffs ranged from 26% on India to 46% on Vietnam, the senior currency analyst says. Also, the Philippines received a 17% increase and Singapore got a 10% increase, which MUFG Bank had thought were the two countries least likely to be targeted in Asia, weren't spared, he says. Meanwhile, Canada and Mexico appear to be exempt from the tariffs for now, at least until perhaps a review at a subsequent date on USCMA-compliant trade, he adds. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0133 GMT - Increased U.S. tariffs on China overnight could lead to zero trade surplus from China, ANZ Research analyst Zhaopeng Xing says. The impact of the new tariffs on China's current trade surplus will be equivalent to about 1.5 percentage points of its GDP, he adds. Considering that this year's general deficit was risen, the tariffs may not affect China's 5% GDP growth goal. However, the levies mark a crucial change for global trade and economic structure that may affect trade flows for many years, ANZ says. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0133 GMT - The U.S. tariff rate on Chinese goods, set to rise to around 65% cumulatively with the latest reciprocal tariffs, is expected to reduce China's economic growth by 2.4 percentage points and lower exports growth by 15.4 percentage points, according to Citigroup estimates. The investment bank says the latest tariff hike "is almost a prohibitive level" for U.S.-China trade and the estimation of impact on the Chinese economy has been made before considering any offsetting measures. The latest move is unlikely to bode well for negotiations between the two nations and Beijing's response will be more substantial than for the fentanyl tariffs, Citigroup says in a note. (singaporeeditors@dowjones.com)

0125 GMT - Australia's agriculture, manufacturing and wine exports are at greatest risk of a direct impact from President Trump's sweeping new tariffs, says Emanuel Datt, founder and chief investment officer at Datt Capital. However, a much greater impact on Australia is likely to come from how the tariffs impact countries such as China, Japan and South Korea, which account for a substantial portion of Australia's exports, Datt says. "Although the 10% baseline tariff was less than anticipated, the imposition of far steeper tariffs on countries such as China (54%), Japan (24%) and Vietnam (46%) has raised fears of prolonged economic disruption and intensified uncertainty about the future of global trade," he says. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

data.title: CBOT soybeans to retest support at $10.07-3/4
data.description:
Key points:
  • For more technical analyses, please clickTECH/C

The CBOT soybean May contract (SK25) is expected to retest support at $10.07-3/4 per bushel, as a three-wave cycle from $9.90-3/4 may have completed.

The completion has been confirmed by the deep fall on April 3. Even though this fall was triggered by the news, it is so deep that it has violated the uptrend.

The bounce triggered by the support at $10.07-3/4 is classified as a pullback toward a rising trendline. A break above $10.18 may lead to a gain to $10.24-1/2.

* Wang Tao is a Reuters market analyst for commodities and energy technicals. Views expressed are his own.

** No information in this analysis should be considered as being business, financial or legal advice. Each reader should consult their own professional or other advisers for business, financial or legal advice regarding the products mentioned in the analyses.

data.title: Royal Orchid Hotels Renews Land Lease for Hotel Royal Orchid in Bengaluru, India
data.description:
Royal Orchid Hotels has entered into an agreement with the Karnataka State Tourism Development Corporation for the renewal of the land lease deed of its Hotel Royal Orchid in Bengaluru, India.

The lease has been renewed for a period of 29 years and 6 months, according to a Wednesday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

data.title: Asian Pay Television Trust Refinances Loan Facilities
data.description:
Asian Pay Television Trust completed the refinancing of its offshore and onshore loan facilities, according to a filing with the Singapore Exchange on Wednesday.

The refinancing was completed after the trust received lenders' commitment.

According to an earlier filing, the facilities comprise a term loan facility and a revolving loan facility, fully underwritten by Taipei Fubon Commercial Bank, Cathay United Bank, and Entie Commercial Bank, who are the exclusive mandated lead arrangers.

Around SG$40 million of offshore loan will be moved back to onshore to save interest costs.

Through debt management, the trustee-manager will be able to trim both facilities by about SG$152.6 million.

data.title: Japan to Continue Asking for Exemption From New U.S. Tariffs, Trade Minister Says
data.description:
By Kosaku Narioka

The Japanese government will continue asking the U.S. to exempt the Asian country from new U.S. tariffs, the country's trade minister said.

Yoji Muto, Japan's minister of economy, trade and industry, said Thursday morning at a press briefing that the U.S. imposing tariffs on imports from Japan was very regrettable.

He added that the ministry would examine the details of the new U.S. tariff measures and their impact on the domestic economy.

Write to Kosaku Narioka at kosaku.narioka@wsj.com

data.title: Samsung Electronics Appoints Acting Head of DX Division, COO of MX Business; Shares Decline 3%
data.description:
Samsung Electronics appointed TM Roh as the acting head of the device eXperience (DX) division, in addition to his role as head of mobile eXperience (MX) business.

Won-joon Choi was named chief operating officer of the MX business, while Cheolgi Kim will take over as head of the digital appliances business. The leadership restructuring aims to boost Samsung's DX and MX operations, the electronics products maker said Tuesday.

Shares of Samsung Electronics fell nearly 3% in recent trade on Thursday.

data.title: U.S. Reciprocal Tariffs May Persist as Source of Economic HeadwindsMarket Talk
data.description: U.S. reciprocal tariffs unveiled Wednesday may persist as a source of economic headwinds, Mizuho Securities (Singapore)'s Vishnu Varathan says in an email. These tariffs could also be "inadvertently compounded, exacerbating vulnerabilities to adverse demand shocks," says the head of Macro Research for Asia ex-Japan. "Asia is particularly hard-hit, with Cambodia, Vietnam, Thailand and Indonesia featuring," Varathan says. Also, Taiwan, South Korea, Japan, India, and the EU haven't been spared, while the U.K., Australia and Singapore are least impacted, he says. Hence, pressure on Asia ex-Japan currencies could continue, Varathan adds. USD/THB rises 0.2% to 34.35; USD/PHP edges 0.1% higher to 57.22; USD/MYR gains 0.4% to 4.4720. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Wednesday at 11 PM ET
data.description:
Trump Tariffs Aim to Bring Down Curtain on Era of Globalization

The president wants companies to return production to the U.S., but it won't be easy.

Asian Markets Roiled by 'Liberation Day' Tariffs

Asian equities tumbled as markets awoke to see some of their worst fears come true: a slate of sweeping U.S. tariffs that could inflict economic damage and disrupt industries.

China Services Gauge Shows Acceleration in Activity Expansion

China's services-sector activity expanded at a faster pace in March, thanks to improving demand boosted by government stimulus measures, according to a private survey.

Trump Officials Gave Conflicting Tariff Rates for China. White House Calls the Issue a 'Pointless Aside.'

Confusion reigned even among some White House officials about what rate the approximately $440 billion in Chinese imports would face.

Some GOP Senators Break With Trump to Reject Canada Tariffs

The Senate passed a resolution to undo the emergency declaration the president used to impose the tariffs, but the measure has been blocked in the Republican-controlled House.

What to Know About Trump's Latest Tariffs

President Trump unveiled a new tariff regime, including 10% across-the-board tariffs on all imports and even higher rates for some nations. Here's the latest.

The Stock Market Pain Is Just Getting Started. 'This is How You Sabotage the World's Economic Engine.'

"Liberation Day" wasn't the clearing event investors hopped for. That isn't good news for stocks-in the short run.

Jobs Aren't a Worry-Yet. Tariffs and DOGE Could Upset Things.

Businesses and corporate budgets are under pressure amid uncertainty over President Donald Trump's tariff hikes and federal job cuts.

U.S. Crude Oil Inventories See Large Build

U.S. crude oil inventories increased by 6.2 million barrels last week as imports rose and refineries reduced their capacity use.

Are Muni Bonds Still a Darling on Wall Street? It Depends Who You Ask

For investors facing a tall federal income tax bill, munis may make more sense than holding Treasurys or cash-like money-market funds. But tax-exempt issues linger.

data.title: Trump tariffs pile stress on ailing world economy
data.description:
Key points:
  • Trump tariffs come as global economy already sub-par
  • Measures seen bad for growth, adding to price pressures
  • Long-term questions grow over US role in global economy
  • Fitch sees U.S. tariffs on all imports jumping to 22%

The latest round of U.S. trade tariffs unveiled on Wednesday will sap yet more vigour from a world economy barely recovered from the post-pandemic inflation surge, weighed down by record debt and unnerved by geopolitical strife.

Depending on how President Donald Trump and leaders of other nations proceed now, it may also go down as a turning point for a globalised system that until now had taken for granted the strength and reliability of America, its largest component.

"Trump's tariffs carry the risk of destroying the global free trade order the United States itself has spear-headed since the Second World War," said Takahide Kiuchi, executive economist at Nomura Research Institute.

But in coming months it will be the plain and simple price-hiking - and therefore demand-dampening - effects of new levies applied to thousands of goods bought and sold by consumers and businesses across the planet that will prevail.

"I see it as a drift of the U.S. and global economy towards worse performance, more uncertainty and possibly heading towards something we could call a global recession," said Antonio Fatas, macroeconomist at the INSEAD business school in France.

Speaking in the White House Rose Garden, Trump said he would impose a 10% baseline tariff on all imports and held up a chart showing higher duties on some of the country's biggest trading partners, including 34% on China and 20% on the European Union.

A 25% auto and auto parts tariff was confirmed earlier. Trump said the tariffs would return strategically vital manufacturing capabilities to the United States.

Under the new global levies imposed by Trump, the U.S. tariff rate on all imports jumped to 22% - a rate last seen around 1910 - from just 2.5% in 2024, said Olu Sonola, head of U.S. economic research at Fitch Ratings.

"This is a game changer, not only for the U.S. economy but for the global economy," Sonola said. "Many countries will likely end up in a recession."

IMF Managing Director Kristalina Georgieva told a Reuters event this week she did not see global recession for now. She added the Fund expected shortly to make a small downward "correction" to its 2025 forecast of 3.3% global growth.

But the impact on national economies is set to diverge widely, given the spectrum of tariffs ranging from 10% for Britain to 49% for Cambodia.

If the result is a wider trade war, that would have even larger repercussions for producers like China, which would be left hunting for new markets in the face of weak domestic consumption.

"Asian economies will be hit harder than most by U.S. reciprocal tariffs," said Marcel Thieliant, head of Asia-Pacific at Capital Economics. "Not only do Asian economies face higher tariffs than many others, they are also more dependent on U.S. goods demand than most."

If the tariffs push the U.S. itself towards recession, that will weigh heavily on developing countries whose fortunes are closely tied to those of the world's largest economy.

Stocks dived and investors scrambled to the safety of bonds, gold and the yen on Thursday on heightened economic worries.

AN 'INVERTED WORLD'

The knock-on effects for central banks and governments are also potentially large.

An unravelling of supply chains that for years kept a lid on prices for consumers could lead to a world in which inflation tends to run "hotter" than the 2%, which central bankers currently agree is a manageable target.

That would complicate decisions for the Bank of Japan, which may face pressure to combat inflation with more rate hikes just as its major counterparts eye cuts, and as its export sector takes a hit from U.S. duties.

But there is little countries can do beyond mitigating the economic hit and asking Washington for exemptions. Auto exporters Japan, hit with a 24% reciprocal tariff rate, and South Korea, which was imposed a 25% rate, have signalled plans to take emergency measures to support affected businesses.

Despite being U.S. allies, both countries were singled out by Trump as the worst offenders of unfair trade practices.

Japan's trade minister warned the U.S. levies could violate World Trade Organization (WTO) rules, but said only that Tokyo will consider "various options" on how to respond. "Given its strong national security ties with the United States, Japan has no cards to play to win concessions," said Nomura's Kiuchi.

Australia's prime minister criticised the tariffs as not "the act of a friend" but ruled out reciprocal measures.

Weak economic output will leave some governments struggling even more to pay down the world's record $318 trillion debt load and find money for budget priorities such as defence, climate action and welfare.

And what if the tariffs do not bring about Trump's aim of private sector investment in domestic U.S. manufacturing, given the domestic labour shortages already facing a country with close to full employment?

Some see him seeking other ways to remove the U.S. global trade deficit that riles him so much - for example by demanding others join in a re-balancing of foreign exchange rates to benefit U.S. exporters.

"We are going to continue to see him putting out there potentially more risky ways of dealing with the continuous strength of the dollar," said Freya Beamish, chief economist at investment strategy firm TS Lombard.

Such moves could jeopardise the privileged position of the dollar as the world reserve currency of choice - an outcome few predict, if only because there are no real alternatives to the dollar.

Nonetheless, European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde on Wednesday told an event in Ireland that Europe needed to act now and accelerate economic reforms to compete in what she called an "inverted world".

"Everyone benefited from a hegemon, the United States, that was committed to a multilateral, rules-based order," she said of the post-Cold War era of low inflation and growing trade in an open global economy.

"Today we must contend with closure, fragmentation and uncertainty."

data.title: Bangladesh's RPGCL seeks 3 LNG cargoes for May delivery, document shows
data.description:
Bangladesh's Rupantarita Prakritik Gas Co has issued a tender seeking three spot cargoes of liquefied natural gas for delivery in May, according to a tender document on its website.

RPGCL is seeking the LNG cargoes for delivery on May 1-2, 10-11 and 16-17, in a tender that will close on April 6, according to the document.

data.title: Stealth Group Holdings to Launch Own-Label Consumables Brand Rivo; Shares Hit All-Time High
data.description:
Stealth Group Holdings will launch Rivo, an own-label brand of consumable products to be sold via high-traffic channels such as petrol and convenience outlets, according to a Thursday filing with the Australian bourse.

The first product category under the Rivo brand will be Rivo Safety, co-developed with PIP Global Safety, per the filing. The product line will offer safety and light industrial essentials such as protection gear, first aid kits, padlocks, and small tools.

Stealth Group was at an all-time high in early afternoon trade on Thursday, with shares up 18% in recent trade.

data.title: Iron ore dips on US tariffs, but resilient steel demand caps losses
data.description:
Iron ore futures inched lower on Thursday after U.S. President Donald Trump unveiled a broad set of reciprocal tariffs, although seasonal demand for the steelmaking ingredient helped cushion the fall.

The most-traded May iron ore contract on China's Dalian Commodity Exchange (DCE) was down 0.2% at 789.5 yuan ($108.12) a metric ton, as of 0240 GMT.

The benchmark May iron ore (SZZFK5) on the Singapore Exchange was 0.79% lower at $102 a ton.

U.S. tariffs were more aggressive than expected and that will weigh on ferrous markets, broker Galaxy Futures said in a note.

Trump on Wednesday revealed a 10% minimum tariff on most goods imported to the United States, with much higher duties on products from dozens of countries, worsening a trade war that threatens to drive up inflation and stall U.S. and worldwide economic growth.

Chinese imports will be hit with a 34% tariff, bringing the total new levy to 54%.

Beijing on Thursday urged the United States to immediately cancel its latest tariffs and vowed countermeasures to safeguard its own interests.

Still, steelmakers have ramped up production during the peak construction season in March and April, cushioning the fall in prices.

"Spot buying at ports in China is said to be brisk as construction activity picks up," said ANZ analysts, adding that manufacturers are more confident in the downstream demand for steel.

"The demand for imported iron ore in China is expected to remain strong in April, as the recovery of steel consumption ... will encourage steelmakers to lift their hot metal output further," said consultancy Mysteel in a note.

Other steelmaking ingredients on the DCE languished, with coking coal NYMEX:ACT1! and coke (DCJcv1) down 0.6% and 0.31%, respectively.

Most steel benchmarks on the Shanghai Futures Exchange posted losses. Rebar eased 0.03%, hot-rolled coil weakened 0.33%, stainless steel fell 1%, while wire rod (SWRcv1) inched up 0.24%.

($1 = 7.3018 yuan)

data.title: Standard Chartered Lifts China Q1 Growth Forecast
data.description:
Standard Chartered raised its 2025 growth forecast for China to 4.8% from 4.5% due to stronger-than-expected real activity and purchasing managers' indexes in the first quarter.

Standard Chartered said China's real gross domestic product growth likely eased to 5.2% in the first quarter, compared with 5.4% in Q4 2024.

data.title: Nanoveu Unit Teams Up with Egypt Research Center on AI Chip Development; Shares Down 6%
data.description:
Nanoveu subsidiary Embedded AI Systems (EMASS) partnered with the Center of Nanoelectronics and Devices in Egypt to create advanced custom integrated circuits using EMASS's ultra-low energy edge artificial intelligence technology, according to a Thursday filing with the Australian bourse.

Under the collaboration, EMASS will develop application-specific integrated circuits through Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company's 16-nanometer fin field-effect transistor process, which uses a 3D transistor architecture, the filing said.

Additionally, Yehia Ismail, Director of the Center of Nanoelectronics and Devices, was named strategic advisor to EMASS as part of the deal, per the filing.

Nanoveu shares were down 6% in afternoon trade Thursday.

data.title: Thai baht/US dollar stronger on Thursday
data.description:
The Thai baht strengthened against the U.S. dollar on Thursday.

At 0303 GMT, the baht was 0.15% higher at 34.36 per dollar, after trading in a range of 34.300 to 34.440. It ended the previous session at 34.41 per dollar, as per LSEG data.
